text: Partial Vaginectomy for Excision of Rectovaginal Endometriosis. This video will demonstrate the excision of rectovaginal endometriosis after completion of a total laparoscopic hysterectomy. We will also describe laparoscopic techniques to perform a partial vaginectomy when the uterus is left in situ. Lastly, we will incorporate surgical techniques and tips in our discussion.

Rectovaginal endometriosis is a form of deep infiltrating endometriosis. When it involves the rectovaginal wall, patients can present with deep dyspareunia and dyskiesia. It commonly affects the posterior fornix and nodularity is often palpable in the posterior cul-de-sac.

The surgeon performs a partial vaginectomy to excise a three-centimeter rectovaginal nodule. The rectovaginal space is developed to mobilize the rectum below the lowest margin of the vaginal nodule before making the colpotomy incision.

The surgeon uses a xeropolysorb suture to tag the distal border of the rectovaginal nodule for localization. Blunt graspers are applied to provide traction and implement the squeeze method, delineating the nodule's margins.

The surgeon identifies a nodule involving only the posterior vaginal wall and creates an inverted V-shaped incision using monopolar shears. The nodule is transected with an anzeal bipolar device, carefully hugging the nodule to minimize excision from the posterior vagina. Traction and counter-traction techniques are applied to achieve complete excision of the nodule.

The surgeon begins the vaginal cuff closure by anchoring a unidirectional barb suture at the apex of the inverted V-shaped incision. The posterior vaginal wall is re-approximated longitudinally in a running fashion, providing additional length. The remaining vaginal cuff is closed transversely, and a modified Richardson stitch using 2-0 PDS is placed at the left vaginal cuff angle to incorporate the left utero-sacral ligament.
The surgeon places a stitch at the right vaginal cuff angle using a 2-0 V-lock suture, running it continuously from right to left to incorporate the entire cut edge of the vaginal mucosa.

The surgeon demonstrates a partial vaginectomy to excise a five-centimeter rectovaginal nodule while leaving the uterus in situ. The endometriotic nodule in the left cul-de-sac is tagged with a single interrupted suture at its most distal border for localization.

The surgeon suspends the uterus to the anterior abdominal wall using a temporary 2-0 Prolene suture, with sterile IV tubing placed between the suture and uterine fundus to achieve uterine antiversion. A temporary left oophorepexy is performed using a 2-0 Prolene suture on a Keith needle, anchoring it to the anterior abdominal wall in the left lower quadrant to facilitate access to the left parametrium.

The surgeon performs ureterolysis due to the nodule's location in the left cul-de-sac and extensive endometriosis in the left ovarian fossa. The ureter is identified along its course amidst severe retroperitoneal fibrosis. Using sharp and blunt dissection, the uterine artery is exposed at its origin, and the pararectal space is developed. To minimize bleeding, the left uterine artery and its vaginal branches are skeletonized and temporarily ligated with 5mm endoclips. The left ureter is then mobilized further laterally.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision medially to develop the left pararectal fossa, followed by a similar incision medial to the right uterosacral ligament to develop the right pararectal fossa. The rectovaginal space is then developed, and the rectum is mobilized off the endometriotic nodule and posterior vagina using both sharp and blunt dissection techniques.

A Bryski-Navratol retractor is placed in the posterior vagina to facilitate exposure, and a colpotomy is initiated using monopolar shears. The surgeon carefully mobilizes the endometriotic nodule off the posterior cervix, identifying the previously tagged suture. Traction and countertraction are applied to nucleate the nodule from its attachment using a bipolar device.

The surgeon reconstructs the large colpotomy incision by reattaching it to the patient's posterior cervix. A two-layer closure is performed using a unidirectional barbed suture in a running fashion, with the first layer incorporating the cut edge of the vaginal mucosa and the second layer serving as an imprecating layer. The previously placed endoclips are removed, and excellent hemostasis is observed.

Partial vaginectomy for excision of rectovaginal endometriosis can be performed conservatively with the uterus left in situ or after completion of the hysterectomy to allow for excellent exposure. Tagging the nodule vaginally can help delineate the borders to aid in dissection. Temporary suspension of the uterus and ovaries to the abdominal wall can improve exposure when a uterine manipulator is not used. Advanced laparoscopic suturing techniques are necessary to reconstruct the vagina.
